content: Sashiko points out an issue in existing code (while reviewing Lyude 's patchset): https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260317213003.606088-1-lyude%40redhat.com
It is interesting that it also figures out that Lyude's later patch fixes the bug

I don't understand some of the slop coming back from this tbh:
"How can a Safe Rust driver export an early GEM object to userspace after the device registers? Because the Ctx is permanently encoded in the object's type, an object created early remains Object<T, Uninit>, When the driver attempts to use requires the trait bound D: drm: :Driver<object<Registered> = Self>."
It can't, because that's not how the API works. Even if it did, the upcast would be fine because if we can suddenly export the gem object to userspace that already implies registration
Yeah, this is definitely nonsense. Overall I find that the review is generally useful however it lacks Rust specific understandings. It does not know the context pattern that we're using.

"Does this type definition permanently lock the object to the Uninit typestate?
When accessing the device via the object later, calling .dev() will return
&Device<T::Driver, Uninit>. Since Device<..., Uninit> does not implement
Deref<Target = T::Data>, the driver cannot safely access its private data
through the device pointer.
Would it be possible to provide a safe API (such as an into_registered()
or assume_ctx() method) to allow upgrading the object's context once the
device is registered?
Without a safe way to transition the typestate, drivers might be forced to
use unsafe blocks to interact with their early GEM objects in a registered
context." As well
it's neat it found a bug but the impression I get is that this is probably not something I should be looking closely at
It can catch some un-intended mistakes pretty well: https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260316150720.1646109-1-gary%40kernel.org
But something design-wise, I won't rely on it
I just don't use tools like this :)
It takes time to verify all of these things
Fair enough, it just comes down to personal choice

just be mindful of how much time it is taking for verifying stuff. The tricky thing about stuff like this is you can often waste more time trying to prove/disprove things it gives you then you'd have saved by just looking through stuff yourself.
I'll look at the top thing it mentions today, or Monday. But human review would be really appreciated. Trying to figure out if I've still got the energy to work today, this week has been pretty awful
yeah - the AI was correct on the very first issue I believe. going to write up and send out a fix for it

It seems like the best fix is just going to be splitting the data_is_init addition into a separate patch, because once we call __drm_dev_alloc() there's not really any sensible way that we can drop the DRM device without release() being called
